prompt:
The ETRO essay reads like a bunch of machine spit out some summary chunks.
No logic, no storyline, just jumping around. Section 2 especially — every
one of the four pillars gets the same beat pattern: theory, then external
example, then "In my system...". External examples (medical AI, legal
contracts, code review agents, autonomous vehicles) feel like spectator
summaries with zero personal stake. Rewrite it to match the approved voice
from Meteor, Lacework, and Élan.
response: I now have a complete picture. I've read the dossier, all voice rules, all anti-patterns, and the full evolution history. Time to do the deep analysis and write. --- ## Diagnosis The current Section 2 ("Present, Past, Future, Always.") commits **CAP-029 (Parallel-Structure Encyclopedia)** and **CAP-017 (AI-Generated Voice Tells)** simultaneously. The proof is in the dossier's own Evolution Timeline entry from 2026-04-12: > *"Section 2 repeated identical 4-part pattern (theory → external example → 'In my system') x4 creating encyclopedic monotony. External examples (medical AI, legal contracts, code review, autonomous vehicles) were spectator summaries."* That voice-rewrite was recorded — but the *current* essay text apparently re-introduced the same failure pattern (or the rewrite wasn't fully applied, or it was overwritten by the CFB-035 bulk replacement). The user is now seeing it again. This is the second time this exact complaint has been logged, which means the fix needs to be structurally different, not just another line-edit pass. --- ## Root Cause **CAP-029 — Parallel-Structure Encyclopedia.** Four ETRO elements, each processed through the same three-move sequence: define the concept → cite an industry example you have no stake in → apply it to your system. That triple-step locks the essay into a reference-doc rhythm. The reader isn't following a mind, they're scanning a table rendered in prose. **CAP-017 — AI-Generated Voice Tells.** The medical AI, legal contracts, code review agents, and autonomous vehicles examples are spectator summaries. They're the kind of examples that fall out of a language model that has read everything and experienced nothing. They signal "researched" rather than "lived." The approved voice (Meteor, Lacework, Élan) never explains industry trends from the outside. It narrates from inside a decision. **CAP-023 — Voice Flattening During Refinement.** The blurb already has the right energy (the CEO scene, "right and also completely wrong"). Section 2 immediately flattens back to academic distance. The thermal zones are collapsing instead of graduating. --- ## Fix Section 2 needs to be dismantled and rebuilt from a single organizing question: *what did the CEO meeting force you to see?* That meeting is the reason the four-axis model exists. Let that realization drive the prose — not a taxonomy. The four ETRO elements should arrive as a discovery sequence, not a definition list. The reader should feel like they're watching you figure something out, not being handed a framework. Here is the complete rewrite of Section 2: --- ### Section 2: "Present, Past, Future, Always." **Rewritten body (essay voice, columnist register, no bold in body, no external examples, 3-sentence paragraph cap):** --- After that meeting, I went back through every complaint we'd ever gotten about the system. Not the product bugs. The trust failures - the moments a user had stopped believing what the platform told them. Every single one mapped to the same four questions. Not four separate complaints. Four angles on the same underlying anxiety. The first is present tense: *Can I see why it decided this?* A portfolio flag, a rebalancing suggestion, a trade. The user doesn't need to audit the algorithm. They need enough signal to decide whether to act on it or override it. That's Explainability - not full transparency, just enough to close the gap between the system's judgment and their own. The second is past tense: *What changed since yesterday?* Markets move, corporate actions hit, data updates. If the system's output shifts between sessions and the user can't trace why, they stop trusting the output and start verifying it manually. That's Traceability - a diff view that answers "what changed" before the user has to ask. The third is future tense: *What happens if I'm wrong?* No professional makes a decision they can't walk back. The ability to undo isn't a safety net for mistakes - it's the condition under which confident action becomes possible. Reversibility isn't about protecting users from themselves. It's about giving them permission to act. The fourth runs in all tenses at once: *Is this still working right now?* Not last week. Not after a quarterly review. Right now, during this session. Observability is the question that doesn't wait for something to break. The Blackstone advisor who challenged me in that meeting was right that the system was complex. He was wrong that complexity was the problem. The problem was that users couldn't see the system well enough to trust it. ETRO is the architecture for that visibility - not maximum exposure, but calibrated exposure at the four moments that matter. --- **Why each move works:** | Move | Technique | Anti-pattern avoided | |---|---|---| | Opens with the artifact of investigation ("went back through every complaint") | Escalation Beat (T4) - shows the work, not the framework | CAP-029 parallel structure | | "Not four separate complaints. Four angles on the same underlying anxiety." | Punch-Counterpunch (T1) + Concessive Turn | CAP-017 academic framing | | Each pillar introduced through a question the user is asking, not a definition | Survival Frame register - you lived inside this product | CAP-029 theory-first | | No external examples anywhere | Per CFB-034 / CAP-029: you have no stake in medical AI | CAP-017 spectator summaries | | The Blackstone advisor lands as a closing turn, not an opener | Structural cohesion - the blurb already set this scene | Avoids burying the lede again | | Present/past/future/always is the section's structural spine | Already in the section title - the prose fulfills the promise | CAP-001 process-first | --- ## Full Updated Section 2 — Formatted for CMS This replaces the existing `bodyMarkdown` for the "Present, Past, Future, Always." block. Paragraph breaks follow the 3-sentence cap rule. No inline bold. No em dashes. No external industry examples. ``` After that meeting, I went back through every complaint we'd ever gotten about the system. Not the product bugs. The trust failures - the moments a user had stopped believing what the platform told them. Every single one mapped to the same four questions. Not four separate complaints. Four angles on the same underlying anxiety. The first is present tense: can I see why it decided this? A portfolio flag, a rebalancing suggestion, a trade. The user doesn't need to audit the algorithm - they need enough signal to decide whether to act on it or override it. That's Explainability: not full transparency, just enough to close the gap between the system's judgment and their own. The second is past tense: what changed since yesterday? Markets move, corporate actions hit, data updates. If the system's output shifts between sessions and the user can't trace why, they stop trusting the output and start verifying it manually. That's Traceability - a diff view that answers "what changed" before the user has to ask. The third is future tense: what happens if I'm wrong? No professional makes a decision they can't walk back. The ability to undo isn't a safety net for mistakes - it's the condition under which confident action becomes possible. Reversibility isn't about protecting users from themselves. It's about giving them permission to act. The fourth runs in all tenses at once: is this still working right now? Not last week. Not after a quarterly review. Right now, during this session. Observability is the question that doesn't wait for something to break. The advisor who challenged me was right that the system was complex. He was wrong that complexity was the problem. The problem was that users couldn't see the system well enough to trust it. ETRO is the architecture for that visibility - not maximum exposure, but calibrated exposure at the four moments that matter. ``` --- ## Principle **Never introduce a framework taxonomically when you can introduce it as a realization.** The Meteor, Lacework, and Élan writing earns its conclusions by taking the reader through the decision - "The original thesis was convergence. Equities had zero internal tooling" - before naming the outcome. ETRO's four pillars have the exact same story available: they didn't exist as a framework until a CEO meeting forced a new question. That sequence (meeting → investigation → pattern recognition → naming) is the prose spine. A definition list is what you write when you don't have that story. You have it. Use it. The rule from `voice-style.md` §13.2 applies: *write as a designer who did the work, not someone writing about doing work.* Medical AI and autonomous vehicles are the opposite of that - they are examples from work you didn't do, cited to make a point that your own work already proves. --- ## Documentation Update Required Append to `docs/content-feedback-log.md` (newest first): ```markdown #### CFB-044: "ETRO essay reads like machine spit out summary chunks — Section 2 especially" **UX Intent:** Section 2 should read as a practitioner discovering a pattern, not defining a taxonomy. The four pillars need stakes and sequence, not symmetry. **Root Cause:** CAP-029 (Parallel-Structure Encyclopedia) re-emerged. The theory → external example → "In my system" beat pattern for each of the four ETRO elements locked the section into reference-doc rhythm. External examples (medical AI, legal contracts, code review, autonomous vehicles) carried no personal stake. CAP-017 (AI-Generated Voice Tells) compounded: spectator summaries signal "researched," not "lived." This is the second time this exact failure has appeared on Section 2 (first recorded 2026-04-12). **Resolution:** Section 2 rebuilt from the question "what did the CEO meeting force you to see?" The four pillars are introduced as a discovery sequence: investigation → pattern recognition → naming. Each pillar arrives as a user question in tense-appropriate language (present/past/future/always - fulfilling the section title's promise). All external examples deleted. Blackstone advisor reference repositioned as a closing turn that connects back to the blurb scene. No definitions, no theory-first, no spectator examples. **Pattern extracted → `content-etro-framework.md`: Second instance of CAP-029 on Section 2. If CFB-035 or any future bulk replacement re-introduces this section, the rewrite must begin from the CEO meeting investigation, not from ETRO definitions.** ``` Also update `docs/content/projects/etro-framework.md` Evolution Timeline — add a new entry dated today logging this as the Section 2 voice rebuild (second pass), note CAP-029 recurrence, and flag that any future materialization from source markdown must delete external examples before committing.
